## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

80257e119 jjb:1011-8a43ab7d:₢A_AAF:n: Reconstruct SA chain and reliquary on canest2bhm100003 after the swap-and-defer in ₢A_AAo left the new depot bare. The depot regen via rbw-dL provisioned the project + GAR repo + GCS bucket but did not chain forward to governor mantle, retriever invest, director invest, reliquary inscribe, or vessel yoke — all canonical post-levy steps per the Payor and Governor handbooks. Service suite's first run after the swap surfaced this as rbtdrc_jwt_governor failing with 'Invalid grant: account not found' (governor SA literally absent on 100003 since 100002's governor was destroyed during rbw-dU teardown), then rbtdrc_hallmark_lifecycle failing with 'Registry preflight failed — 6 of 6 reliquary tool images missing from GAR' (reliquary stamp r260512130920 yoked into all 9 rbrv.env files but never inscribed onto 100003). Repair sequence executed: (1) rbw-aM minted governor-202605131246, (2) rbw-arI t3r minted retriever, (3) rbw-adI t3d minted director — first attempt failed with HTTP 403 on artifactregistry.repositories.getIamPolicy ~30s after governor mantle (IAM propagation race documented in the memo committed here), divest+re-invest cleared it, (4) rbw-dI inscribed reliquary r260513125123 (5min 6s Cloud Build, 6 tool images: gcloud, docker, alpine, syft, binfmt, skopeo), (5) rbw-dY yoked the new stamp across all 9 vessel rbrv.env files. RBRA credential files (governor, retriever, director) live in ../station-files/secrets/ outside the tracked tree, so this commit captures only the rbrv.env churn from yoke + the propagation-race memo. Service suite re-run is in flight in the background to validate the reconstructed substrate. Memo characterizes the GAR-IAM 403 as an asymmetric propagation lag — same governor token successfully wrote project-scope and SA-scope IAM seconds before, but AR-resource-scope IAM cache hadn't caught up; the script's existing SA-key propagation retry doesn't cover this call site. Out-of-scope repair vectors (propagation-retry wrapper around getIamPolicy/setIamPolicy on AR, or a deliberate post-mantle wait covering AR-IAM cache) noted for future work.

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Collision-aware diagnostic
  
  Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  - BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Collision-aware diagnostic
  
  Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  - BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85

# jjezs_paddock ₣BB

## Context

Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.

This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.

## Single operator prerequisite chain

Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.

## Entry contract — load-bearing

`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:

- Working tree clean
- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

## Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage

The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.

Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.

**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.

Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.

- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. ## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

content.text:
**First surprise** — and it's the exact bug class this heat exists to catch.

**`rbtdro_onboarding_kludge_tadmor` failed** because `debian:bookworm-slim` (sentry-tadmor's pass-through base image) is missing from the local docker cache, and `rbob_kludge_sentry` refuses to fetch it:

```
WARNING: Kludge cannot proceed — base image(s) not cached locally
  Kludge runs uncredentialed and never reaches a registry. All declared
  base images must be in the local docker cache before kludge runs.

  Origin slots (docker pull from upstream):
    docker pull debian:bookworm-slim
```

**Where in the suite**: `enrollment-validation` 47/47, `pristine-lifecycle` 5/5, `canonical-establish` 4/4 all green; failure is at case 2 of `onboarding-sequence` (right after `inscribe-reliquary` passed). So canonical depot + governor/retriever/director SAs are live in GCP; reliquary is inscribed.

**Triage call**: not a one-liner. The handbook walk (`rbw-Odf` conjure track for tadmor) is exercising `rbob_kludge_sentry` against a pristine docker cache, which structurally cannot work given kludge's "uncredentialed, no registry" contract. Three plausible directions:

1. **Onboarding handbook prepends an explicit `docker pull` step** for declared origin slots — keeps kludge contract intact, makes the prerequisite operator-visible.
2. **Onboarding fixture pre-pulls base images** before each kludge case — handbook stays as is but fixture seeds the cache; risks masking the same gap for future operators.
3. **Onboarding-sequence case ordering rethink** — tadmor's `rbw-Ofc` track produces the kludge; maybe the handbook track itself should have a "pull bases" step that the fixture exercises.

Per docket this is "Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding."

Per failure-mode contract: no patch-and-continue. Whichever direction we pick, the next attempt starts from a fresh `rbw-MZ`. Current state: one canonical depot + SAs + reliquary live (no-teardown decision applies on success only; under fail-fast the operator drives cleanup).

653598630 jjb:1011-c42009d5:₢A_AAb:n: EXPERIMENT (revert after observation): rename compose network 'transit' → 'auplink' so it sorts alphabetically before 'enclave'. Memo memo-20260512-srjcl-port-publish-regression.md asserts Docker's port-publish target is the alphabetically-first attached network — extrapolated from BBABE's empirically-established alphabetical rule for default-route assignment, but not independently verified for port-publish. This experiment falsifies-or-confirms that extrapolation as a binary outcome: if alphabetical hypothesis holds, srjcl jupyter-connectivity goes green (HTTP 200) with the renamed network because the shipped DNAT (-i ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}) and RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT (-i uplink -o enclave) rules now match the path Docker actually delivers packets on; if false, curl stays at HTTP 000 and we need a different theory of the regression. Two files touched: (1) .rbk/rbob_compose.yml renames the network at top-level declaration (line 99) and at sentry's services.networks ref (line 33), plus header-comment update; (2) Tools/rbk/rbob_bottle.sh:345 collision-guard allowlist swaps 'transit' → 'auplink' so the guard recognises the renamed network as ours. No image rebuild required — sentry's rbjs_sentry.sh does interface discovery by IP (against RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP), never reads docker network names, so the baked entrypoint is unaffected. Test: tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.srjcl.sh; expected to flip from HTTP 000 (broken in baseline) to HTTP 200 if alphabetical-ordering hypothesis is causal for port-publish target selection. 6d836814c jjb:1011-048edf10:₢BBABC:n: Tighten charge invariant with label-based nameplate-collision scan; align compose YAML and spec quoins with current ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}_* network naming. Surfaced by ₣BB pristine run on this host where pre-prefix-era residue (project=tadmor, label-only) blocked tadmor charge after MZ — the existing subnet-equality scan in zrbob_detect_subnet_conflict couldn't see foreign compose projects whose networks carry our nameplate but a different project name. (1) New zrbob_detect_nameplate_collision function in rbob_bottle.sh enumerates networks bearing both com.docker.compose.project and com.docker.compose.network labels, filters to those whose network label is enclave|transit AND project label is bare RBRN_MONIKER OR ends with -RBRN_MONIKER (covers pre-prefix-era residue and any prefix form, since RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX is constrained by rbrr_regime.sh:100 to end in '-'); refusal enumerates each offending network with the exact docker rm + docker network rm cleanup commands. Existing zrbob_detect_subnet_conflict retained verbatim as defense-in-depth for non-compose networks (manual docker network create) that bypass label-based detection. Both detectors wired into rbob_charge between compose-down and compose-up, with a comment documenting the layered design. (2) YAML rename in .rbk/rbob_compose.yml: sentry's services.networks.default → transit, top-level networks.transit declared alongside networks.enclave (eliminates compose's implicit default), header comment updated. (3) Spec sweep: RBS0 quoins at_transit_network_name and at_enclave_network_name now describe ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}_transit and _enclave (was just ${RBRN_MONIKER}_*, broken since the ZRBOB_PROJECT pre-prefix fix); RBSNC/RBSNX replace bare 'default' references with {at_transit_network} quoin links; RBSCC step 3 retitled 'Detect Nameplate Collisions' and split into label-based and subnet-based sub-steps; RBSSS parenthetical 'default' → 'transit'. BCG audit on new function applied: local → local -r for assigned-once locals; per-iteration synthesized vars declared once outside the for loop and reassigned inside (avoids readonly redeclaration); stderr captured to forensic temp files (no 2>/dev/null) with paths in die messages; empty-array guard `(( ${#z_names[@]} ))` around value iteration per set-u safety; inverted `test ... && return 0` to `test ... -gt 0 || return 0`; quoted inner expansion in parameter operator `${z_proj_label%-"${RBRN_MONIKER}"}`. Verified: bash -n clean, shellcheck -S error clean, fast suite green (4 fixtures, 98 cases). Open questions resolved: keep project-scoped compose-down as fast-path before broader scan (proper compose-aware teardown of own project); accept the *-{moniker} false-positive risk (refusal diagnostic is operator-friendly enough).
bb6154271 jjb:1011-854eda41:₢BBAAX:n: Repair charge-after-ordain failure (BBAAX surface 2 — moriah charge died on 'Sentry vouch artifact missing locally' after onboarding-sequence completed). Two coordinated edits implementing Path A (charge auto-bootstraps when arks aren't local). (1) rbtdro_onboarding.rs: removed two of three docker rmi sites that contradict their own docstrings and RBSAS summon spec. ordain_conjure_impl line 812 (image+about+vouch) blocked moriah/srjcl/pluml — these consume the sentry-tether hallmark per RBTDRO_CONSUMERS_SENTRY_TETHER but the rmi removed the just-summoned arks, so 'Abjure is omitted because downstream crucible fixtures consume this hallmark' was a lie. ordain_bind_impl line 1114 (image only) similarly blocked pluml. ordain_graft's rmi (line 1217) kept since graft has no consumers and the rmi is paired with explicit abjure for self-contained round-trip cleanup. ordain_bind switched from rbtdro_ordain_capture_full to rbtdro_ordain_capture since gar_root/ark_stem are no longer needed (parallel to ordain_airgap shape). (2) rbob_bottle.sh: rbob_charge's vouch-missing branches (lines 316-327) replaced with auto-summon via new helper zrbob_summon_full_hallmark, which pulls all 3 arks (image+about+vouch) given a hallmark — mirrors rbfr_summon's all-three-arks semantics inline. Existing image-only zrbob_vouch_gate_and_summon kept as defense-in-depth for partial-state cases (vouch present but image somehow missing). Net: charge becomes self-bootstrapping — 'image accessible → start container' regardless of how arks arrive (kludge=local-build, ordain=GAR), matching the docket's vessel-exists preconditions and the handbook tracks (rbw-Oda goes 'ordain → charge moriah' with no explicit summon between). Verified: rbtd-b clean (after fixing unused image_ref in ordain_bind), bash -n green on rbob_bottle.sh, shellcheck -S error clean. RBS0/RBSAS/RBSAA spec check confirmed no spec mandates local-side cleanup after summon — the rmi was an unspecified implementation choice.
3eda4229d jjb:1011-cd381d97:₢BBAAs:n: Two orthogonal fixes for the BBAAs onboarding-sequence empirical loop, landed together. (A) Three-line nudge in rbob_bottle.sh: at the three internal callsites (rbob_kludge:503, rbob_kludge_sentry:534, rbob_ordain:565) replace `rbfd_kludge "${z_vessel_dir}"` with `BUZ_FOLIO="${RBRN_*_VESSEL}" rbfd_kludge` (and the ordain analog). Drops the now-dead `local -r z_vessel_dir="${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/${RBRN_*_VESSEL}"` plus its `test -d` guard at each site (existence check moves into rbfd's own `zrbfc_resolve_vessel`, single source of truth for vessel resolution diagnostics). Solves kludge-tadmor blocker observed in today's empirical run: trace `Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh: line 185: rbrv_list_capture: command not found` was symptom of `zrbfc_resolve_vessel(BUZ_FOLIO="tadmor")` falling through to listing branch because `tadmor` is the nameplate moniker (inherited from rbw-cKS dispatch into rbob_cli.sh process), not a vessel sigil — rbfd_kludge expected sigil/path. Bash assignment-prefix `VAR=value func` is the language's primitive for scoped env override and is symmetric with the dispatcher's `BUZ_FOLIO="${z_folio}" exec` (buz_zipper.sh:280) — same primitive at finer scope. Preserves BBAAs's universal-BUZ_FOLIO-contract design intent (handlers read BUZ_FOLIO uniformly across all rb*_, jjfp_, buhw_, rbgg_ modules; cli.sh furnishes also read BUZ_FOLIO for kindle); the rbob → rbfd internal calls just needed to honor the contract by setting BUZ_FOLIO to rbob's natural-derivative (RBRN_*_VESSEL sigil) for the inner call. After this patch, BUZ_FOLIO usage is uniform: dispatcher writes once via exec, callers may scope-override via assignment-prefix, all consumers read. Zero handler/dispatcher edits needed. (B) Strip --force flag from rbfl_jettison and rbfl_abjure plus their RBSIJ/RBSAA specs and 6 Rust orchestrator callsites in rbtdrc_crucible.rs — origin trace: docket revision 260128-2116 for ₢APAAT (silks `add-image-delete-by-ark`) introduced 'Confirm before deletion or require --force flag' as freelance docket text never operator-authorized; subsequent reslating preserved it; alignment commit 525b7fac (`:A:` Feb 9 2026) pulled `(add confirmation/force)` into the spec; bd344f92 (Haiku sub-agent, same day) implemented z_force/z_skip_confirm/--force doc-param mechanically; 388dd454 carried it through the rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh extraction; 7167b795 (BBAAs) included `${2:-}` → `${1:-}` shift specifically because of --force. Removed: `local z_force="${1:-}"`, `buc_doc_param "--force" "..."`, `local z_skip_confirm=false` + `if test "${z_force}" = "--force"` branch, and the `if test "${z_skip_confirm}" = "false"` wrapping around `buc_require` — `buc_require` runs unconditionally (single coherent path). The codebase already had a non-load-bearing-correct skip mechanism (`BURE_CONFIRM=skip` env var checked at buc_require:335-338, buc_command.sh) — --force was a redundant duplicate variant. Updated all 6 Rust orchestrator callsites in rbtdrc_crucible.rs (lines 2603, 2688, 2720, 2820, 2948, 3003) from `&[&hallmark, "--force"], &[]` to `&[&hallmark], &[("BURE_CONFIRM", "skip")]` — fixtures continue to auto-confirm via the existing env-var bypass; --force literal disappears from the entire rbk tree (verified via grep). Spec edits: RBSIJ-image_jettison.adoc:51-59 collapses 'Unless --force flag is provided' nested-step into unconditional 'Confirm Jettison' step; RBSAA-ark_abjure.adoc:84-92 same shape. Verification: bash -n green on rbob_bottle.sh and rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh; tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh green (theurge dev profile, 1.76s, no Rust warnings); tt/rbw-tf.QualifyFast.sh green (tabtargets, RBW colophon registrations, generated context freshness all OK); tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh 25/26 with rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels pre-existing fail unchanged from BBAA3/BBAA4/BBAAs baseline (carrying-forward marshal-zero validator surfacing) — no new failures introduced. Empirical re-run of onboarding-sequence pending operator go-ahead per docket iteration loop. Deferred follow-ups (separate notches when relevant): (i) source rbrv_regime.sh in zrbob_furnish to make rbrv_list_capture available in failure-listing branches reached from rbob processes (currently latent, surfaces only on resolution-failure paths and obscures real diagnostics); (ii) audit other Rust orchestrator/handbook callers passing positional args to BUZ_FOLIO-reading handlers — discovery recipe `grep -rn "rbtdri_invoke_global\|rbtdri_invoke_global_imprint" Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/` cross-referenced against zipper channel column for any handler with internal bash callers in rb*.sh modules. Caveat: scoped to the empirical failure trace and the unauthorized-flag scrub; if any handler outside this bug class has a similar internal-caller mismatch, will surface on the next empirical run as a new failure to diagnose.

Found it. The "critical step" wasn't lost — a **new guard was added without the matching upstream-pull step**.

Commit `a21715436` (₢BAAAI, recent) added a presence guard to `rbfd_kludge` that refuses if base images aren't in the local docker cache:

> "buc_die on any miss before docker is invoked — kludge stays uncredentialed by deferring the credentialed fetch out-of-band to wrest/pull"

The commit explicitly **deferred** the cold-cache verification:

> "Operator-side gates from the docket (cold-cache anchored vessel kludge, crucible suite GREEN) **deferred per ₣BA paddock's fast-only test-suite reservation — surfaced for operator action when ₣A_'s parallel-period burn-in clears**."

So the design is intentional: kludge stays uncredentialed; the pull happens out-of-band. But the corresponding step ("operator/handbook/fixture pulls base images before kludge") was never wired into either the onboarding-sequence fixture or the `rbw-Ofc`/`rbw-Odf` handbook tracks. The gauntlet is the first cold-cache run since the guard landed — exactly the structural gap the deferral was waiting on.

Let me check what the handbook actually prescribes today.

**Tree state**: clean — pristine-lifecycle, canonical-establish, and inscribe-reliquary committed their progress to the cerebro-BB-001 branch; kludge-tadmor failed without committing.

**GCP state**: canonical depot `canest2bhl100008` live with reliquary inscribed; governor/retriever/director SAs live. Per no-teardown decision + fail-fast contract, these persist until you drive cleanup or the next `rbw-MZ` cycle.

**Per failure-mode contract**: next attempt is `rbw-MZ` + fresh `rbw-tP` on a new cerebro branch after the fix lands. No patch-and-continue.

---

**Triage call** — three structural options, ranked by my read:

**Option C (preferred)** — Mint a new tabtarget that pulls all declared origin slots for a vessel (anchored → `rbw-iwe` wrest-enshrined; unanchored → `docker pull`). Single mechanism, both handbook and fixture consume it. Naturally extends the `wrest` family the commit message at `a21715436` itself pointed at: *"deferring the credentialed fetch out-of-band to wrest/pull"*. The tadmor failure is an **unanchored** slot (`debian:bookworm-slim` pass-through), so the new tabtarget would handle both anchored and unanchored cases uniformly.

**Option B** — Handbook tracks gain an explicit `docker pull` step before kludge; fixture exercises the handbook walk. Preserves dual-purpose property. Needs handbook to enumerate origin slots per vessel (currently it doesn't read rbrv.env).

**Option A** — Onboarding-sequence fixture pre-pulls before each kludge case, leaving handbook untouched. Smallest surface but breaks the **dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage** property from the paddock — operators following docs will hit the same wall.

C is the load-bearing answer because `a21715436` itself anticipated this mechanism but deferred it. The deferral note ("surfaced for operator action when ₣A_'s parallel-period burn-in clears") is now ripe.

file.content:
# Resolve base images — mirror conjure's anchor-aware resolution so an enshrined
  # vessel built via kludge resolves the same GAR-anchored layers as conjure.
  # Kludge stays uncredentialed: anchored refs must be pre-wrested into the local
  # cache, origin refs pre-pulled. --pull=never on the build enforces no-network.
  local -r z_gar_repo_base="${RBGD_GAR_LOCATION}${RBGC_GAR_HOST_SUFFIX}/${RBGD_GAR_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}"
  local z_build_args=()
  local z_slot="" z_origin_var="" z_anchor_var="" z_origin="" z_anchor=""
  local z_slot_ref="" z_pkg_path="" z_tag="" z_miss=""
  local z_anchored_misses=()
  local z_origin_misses=()
  for z_slot in 1 2 3; do
    z_origin_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_slot}_ORIGIN"
    z_anchor_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_slot}_ANCHOR"
    z_origin="${!z_origin_var:-}"
    z_anchor="${!z_anchor_var:-}"

    if test -z "${z_origin}" && test -n "${z_anchor}"; then
      buc_die "Malformed regime: ${z_anchor_var}=${z_anchor} set but ${z_origin_var} is empty"
    fi
    test -n "${z_origin}" || continue

    if test -n "${z_anchor}"; then
      case "${z_anchor}" in
        *:*) : ;;
        *)   buc_die "Invalid ${z_anchor_var} locator format (expected package-path:tag): ${z_anchor}" ;;
      esac
      z_pkg_path="${z_anchor%:*}"
      z_tag="${z_anchor##*:}"
      test -n "${z_pkg_path}" || buc_die "Package path is empty in ${z_anchor_var}: ${z_anchor}"
      test -n "${z_tag}"      || buc_die "Tag is empty in ${z_anchor_var}: ${z_anchor}"
      z_slot_ref="${z_gar_repo_base}/${z_pkg_path}:${z_tag}"
      buc_info "Image slot ${z_slot} (anchored): ${z_slot_ref}"
      if ! docker image inspect "${z_slot_ref}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        z_anchored_misses+=("${z_anchor}")
      fi
    else
      z_slot_ref="${z_origin}"
      buc_info "Image slot ${z_slot} (pass-through): ${z_slot_ref}"
      if ! docker image inspect "${z_slot_ref}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        z_origin_misses+=("${z_origin}")
      fi
    fi

    z_build_args+=("--build-arg" "RBF_IMAGE_${z_slot}=${z_slot_ref}")
  done
  test ${#z_build_args[@]} -gt 0 || buc_die "No RBRV_IMAGE_n_ORIGIN found in vessel config"

  if test "${#z_anchored_misses[@]}" -gt 0 || test "${#z_origin_misses[@]}" -gt 0; then
    buc_warn "Kludge cannot proceed — base image(s) not cached locally"
    buc_bare "  Kludge runs uncredentialed and never reaches a registry. All declared"
    buc_bare "  base images must be in the local docker cache before kludge runs."
    buc_bare ""
    if test "${#z_anchored_misses[@]}" -gt 0; then
      buc_bare "  Anchored slots (wrest from GAR):"
      for z_miss in "${z_anchored_misses[@]}"; do
        buc_tabtarget "${RBZ_WREST_ENSHRINED_IMAGE}" "${z_miss}"
      done
      buc_bare ""
    fi
    if test "${#z_origin_misses[@]}" -gt 0; then
      buc_bare "  Origin slots (docker pull from upstream):"
      for z_miss in "${z_origin_misses[@]}"; do
        buc_bare "    docker pull ${z_miss}"
      done
      buc_bare ""
    fi
    buc_die "Local image cache incomplete — see remediation above"
  fi

  # Timestamp for chronological sorting, git describe for commit provenance
  # BURD_GIT_CONTEXT is exported by bud_dispatch; dirty-tree guard above ensures clean tree
  local -r z_hallmark="${RBGC_HALLMARK_PREFIX_KLUDGE}${BURD_NOW_STAMP:2:6}${BURD_NOW_STAMP:9:6}-${BURD_GIT_CONTEXT}"

  # Construct image refs matching compose/vouch-gate format (new layout — hallmark-as-tag).

You're right to push on this. Re-reading the slot loop at `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1391-1448`, the "uncredentialed" property splits cleanly by slot type:

- **Anchored slots** (`RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR` → GAR ref) — pull requires GCP auth. Out-of-band via wrest is the right shape.
- **Origin slots** (pass-through, public upstream like `debian:bookworm-slim`) — `docker pull` needs **no credentials**. The "uncredentialed" framing was over-broad.

The current refusal lumps both together. The clean fix: kludge auto-pulls origin-slot misses inline, keeps the refusal+wrest remediation for anchored-slot misses.

**Code shape** — narrow edit at lines 1419-1448:
- Replace the origin-miss accumulation with an inline `docker pull` (announcing the pull at info level)
- Keep `docker image inspect` re-check after pull as a sanity gate
- Anchored-miss path unchanged — wrest remediation stays
- Update the comment block at 1381-1384 to distinguish the two cases
- `--pull=never` backstop on the build stays (tag-drift defense)

**Why this is safe**:
- In a pristine kludge flow, all slots are unanchored (anchors only land via `ordain conjure`, which doesn't precede kludge) — so auto-pull resolves every observed case
- Functionally equivalent to the operator running the `docker pull <upstream>` that the current diagnostic prescribes — same network reach, just inline
- Teaching moment is preserved if we log the pull (`Auto-pulling origin slot 1: debian:bookworm-slim`)

This is structural-but-narrow — ~15 lines of code, no new tabtarget, no handbook changes, no fixture changes. Per docket I'd slate it as a small follow-up pace for audit trail, then `rbw-MZ` + fresh `rbw-tP` on a new cerebro branch.

# jjezs_slate kludge-origin-slot-auto-pull

## Character

`rbfd_kludge`'s presence guard at `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1391-1448` lumps anchored-slot and origin-slot misses under one "kludge stays uncredentialed" refusal. The framing is over-broad: anchored slots point into GAR and need GCP auth (wrest stays out-of-band, correct); origin slots are public upstream pulls that need no credentials. The over-broad refusal forces operators and fixtures into an extra step that buys nothing.

Surfaced by ₢BBAAX's first gauntlet run: `onboarding-sequence/rbtdro_onboarding_kludge_tadmor` failed in pristine state because `debian:bookworm-slim` (tadmor sentry's only slot, unanchored pass-through) wasn't cached locally. In any pristine kludge flow every slot is unanchored — anchors don't land until an `ordain conjure` — so the operator-facing diagnostic prescribes `docker pull` for every slot, which kludge itself could just do.

## Scope

- `rbfd_kludge` auto-pulls origin-slot misses inline (announcing each pull at info level for teaching/visibility)
- Anchored-slot miss path unchanged: refusal + wrest remediation, preserving the credentialed-out-of-band property
- `--pull=never` backstop on the build stays as tag-drift defense
- Comment block at `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1381-1384` updated to distinguish the two slot types

## Constraints

- No new tabtargets, no handbook edits, no fixture edits
- Origin pull must announce itself — preserve the teaching moment that the current diagnostic provides
- `docker image inspect` re-check after pull stays as a sanity gate before kludge continues

## Cross-pace dependencies

- Blocks ₢BBAAX's next attempt (next `rbw-tP` run after this lands)

## Out of scope

- Anchored-slot ergonomics (separate question, not surfaced by this run)
- `--pull=never` backstop rethink
- Handbook prose update — handbook currently says nothing about pre-pulling, which becomes accurate again once this lands
